questions.question: The GCF of two positive integers is 12, and their LCM is 180. If one of the integers is 36, what is the other integer?
questions.explanation: Use the relationship: GCF(a,b) × LCM(a,b) = a × b. So 12 × 180 = 36 × b, giving 2160 = 36b, thus b = 60. Verify: GCF(36,60) = 12 and LCM(36,60) = 180. ✓

questions.question: A number is divisible by both 8 and 15 but leaves a remainder of 3 when divided by 7. What is the smallest such positive number greater than 100?
questions.explanation: The number must be a multiple of LCM(8,15) = 120. Candidates: 120, 240, 360, … Check remainders mod 7: 120 ÷ 7 = 17 R 1; 240 ÷ 7 = 34 R 2; 360 ÷ 7 = 51 R 3. ✓ Answer is 360. Wait—recalculating: 240 = 34×7 + 2, not 3. 360 = 51×7 + 3. Answer: 360.

questions.question: How many positive divisors does 2^4 × 3^3 × 5^2 have?
questions.explanation: Use the divisor formula: if n = p₁^a₁ × p₂^a₂ × p₃^a₃, then the number of divisors is (a₁+1)(a₂+1)(a₃+1). Here: (4+1)(3+1)(2+1) = 5 × 4 × 3 = 60.

questions.question: Two bells ring at intervals of 18 and 24 minutes respectively. If they ring together at noon, at what time will they next ring together? Express your answer as hours and minutes after noon.
questions.explanation: They ring together every LCM(18,24) minutes. LCM = 72 minutes (since 18 = 2×3² and 24 = 2³×3, LCM = 2³×3² = 72). 72 minutes = 1 hour 12 minutes.

questions.question: A prime number p satisfies the condition that p+6, p+8, and p+12 are all composite. Find the smallest such prime.
questions.explanation: Test small primes: p=2 gives 8(C), 10(C), 14(C)—all composite. But check p=3: 9(C), 11(P)—fails. p=5: 11(P)—fails. p=7: 13(P)—fails. Return to p=2: works but isn't the first to satisfy. Actually, verify p=5: 11 is prime, so fails. Recheck p=2: 8, 10, 14 are all composite. Answer: 2.

questions.question: If 24 is a factor of n and n is a factor of 240, how many possible integer values can n take?
questions.explanation: n must be a multiple of 24 and a divisor of 240. So 24|n and n|240, meaning n ∈ {24, 48, 120, 240}. Actually, list divisors of 240 that are multiples of 24: 24, 48, 80... Better: divisors of 240 divisible by 24 are {24, 48, 120, 240}. Wait—240/24 = 10, so check divisors of 10: 1,2,5,10 give 24, 48, 120, 240. But 80: 240/80=3, and 80/24 not an integer. Divisors: 24, 48, 120, 240 = 4 values. Recalculating: 240 = 2⁴×3×5, 24 = 2³×3. Divisors of 240 containing at least 2³×3 are found by varying the powers of 2 (from 3 to 4) and powers of 3,5. That's 2×2×2 = 8 values.
